past_medical_history: No known medical history — has not seen a physician in approximately 8 years
family_history: Father alive at age 74, hypertension, type 2 diabetes
No family history of immunodeficiency or autoimmune disease

social_history.smoking: Smokes 5-10 cigarettes per day for 20 years
social_history.alcohol: Drinks 2-3 beers most evenings, occasionally more on weekends
social_history.occupation: Warehouse worker for 12 years, does heavy lifting
social_history.living_situation: Recently separated from long-term partner of 15 years. Currently living alone in an apartment. Partner was a man — patient had not previously disclosed sexual orientation to healthcare providers.
social_history.exercise: Active at work but no formal exercise
social_history.illicit_drugs: Denies current use. Used cocaine recreationally in his 20s and early 30s, stopped approximately 10 years ago.
social_history.recent_illness: Oral thrush noted by dentist 2 weeks ago during routine cleaning — prescribed nystatin swish-and-swallow

neurological_exam.mental_status: GCS 14 (E4V4M6). Mildly confused, oriented to person and place but not to date (off by 3 weeks). Speech is slow but fluent. Concentration mildly impaired. Registration 3/3, recall 1/3 at five minutes. No aphasia or dysarthria.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils equal 3 mm, reactive to light bilaterally. Bilateral CN VI palsy — limited lateral gaze bilaterally, more prominent on the right. Visual fields full to confrontation. Bilateral papilledema (grade II) on fundoscopic exam. Facial sensation intact. Facial strength symmetric. Hearing intact bilaterally. Oral thrush present on palate and buccal mucosa. Palate elevates symmetrically. Tongue midline.
neurological_exam.motor: Strength 5/5 in all extremities. Mildly reduced muscle bulk diffusely — generalized wasting. Normal tone.
neurological_exam.sensory: Intact to light touch, pinprick, vibration, and proprioception in all extremities.
neurological_exam.reflexes: 2+ and symmetric in bilateral upper and lower extremities. Plantar responses flexor bilaterally.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose and heel-to-shin intact bilaterally. Rapid alternating movements normal.
neurological_exam.gait: Slow and cautious but steady. Tandem gait performed with one misstep. Romberg negative.
neurological_exam.additional: Notably absent meningismus — no neck stiffness, Kernig sign negative, Brudzinski sign negative. This is a critical finding: meningismus is often absent in cryptococcal meningitis due to the organism's polysaccharide capsule causing less meningeal inflammation. Visual obscurations reported by patient — transient bilateral visual graying lasting seconds, occurring several times per hour, suggestive of raised intracranial pressure. Generalized wasting with temporal muscle wasting bilaterally. Weight loss of approximately 10 kg over 3 months per patient report.

patient.chief_complaint: Worst headache of my life getting worse over 3 weeks
patient.history_present_illness: Mr. Darius Washington is a 48-year-old right-handed African American man who presents to the emergency department with a 4-week history of progressive headache that he describes as 'the worst headache of my life.' The headache began approximately 4 weeks ago as a dull, generalized pressure sensation that he initially attributed to work stress. Over the first two weeks, it gradually intensified and became constant. He tried over-the-counter ibuprofen and acetaminophen with minimal relief. Over the past two weeks, the headache has become severe, particularly in the morning upon waking, and is now accompanied by nausea (no vomiting), blurred vision, and a sense of cognitive slowing. He reports transient episodes of bilateral visual graying lasting several seconds, occurring multiple times per hour for the past 5 days — these visual obscurations are concerning for raised intracranial pressure. He has lost approximately 10 kg over the past 3 months without intentional dieting. He reports night sweats for the past 6 weeks. He was seen by his dentist 2 weeks ago for a routine cleaning, at which time oral thrush was noted and he was prescribed nystatin. He has not seen a doctor in approximately 8 years and has no known medical history. He recently separated from his long-term male partner of 15 years and is currently living alone. He denies any history of HIV testing. He denies intravenous drug use but acknowledges prior recreational cocaine use in his 20s-30s. He denies recent travel, tick exposure, or known sick contacts. He reports decreased appetite and fatigue for the past 2 months. Importantly, he describes the headache as 'gradually building' rather than sudden or thunderclap in onset, which helps distinguish this from subarachnoid hemorrhage despite his description of it being the 'worst headache.'

findings.frequency: 5-7 Hz intermittent theta activity superimposed on a mildly slow background
findings.state: Present during wakefulness, increasing during drowsiness
findings.clinical_correlation: Non-specific finding consistent with mild diffuse encephalopathy

activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: Symmetric photic driving at 10-16 Hz. No photoparoxysmal response.

eeg.impression: Mildly abnormal EEG showing diffuse theta slowing consistent with mild encephalopathy. No epileptiform discharges or electrographic seizures captured during this 30-minute recording.
eeg.limitations: Routine 30-minute recording in a mildly confused patient.
recommended_actions: Clinical correlation with CSF analysis and neuroimaging
Consider continuous EEG if seizures suspected

findings.size: Lateral ventricles mildly dilated with Evans index 0.32 (borderline)

signal_characteristics.T2: Mild periventricular T2 hyperintensity suggesting early transependymal CSF flow

findings.mass_effect: No focal mass effect. Mild effacement of cortical sulci relative to ventricular size.

findings.location: Bilateral basal ganglia — multiple tiny foci in caudate heads and lentiform nuclei bilaterally

additional_observations: No leptomeningeal enhancement (often absent in cryptococcal meningitis)
mri.impression: 1. Communicating hydrocephalus with mild ventriculomegaly. 2. Multiple tiny T2-bright, FLAIR-suppressed foci in bilateral basal ganglia following CSF signal — classic appearance of gelatinous pseudocysts, highly characteristic of cryptococcal infection. 3. No leptomeningeal enhancement, which is a recognized feature of cryptococcal meningitis in immunocompromised patients (the impaired immune response fails to generate enhancing inflammation).

differential_by_imaging.key_features: Basal ganglia gelatinous pseudocysts (dilated perivascular spaces filled with cryptococcal organisms and mucoid material) are pathognomonic for CNS cryptococcosis
While perivascular spaces can be prominent in the basal ganglia, the multiplicity, clinical context, and associated hydrocephalus favor an infectious etiology
No restricted diffusion on DWI; the CSF-signal characteristics favor pseudocysts over infarcts

recommended_actions: Urgent lumbar puncture with opening pressure measurement
CSF cryptococcal antigen, India ink, and fungal culture

ecg.interpretation: Normal sinus rhythm at 78 bpm. No conduction abnormalities. No ST-T wave changes.

CBC.clinical_significance: Leukopenia — raises concern for immunosuppression, particularly HIV/AIDS
Moderate normocytic anemia — consistent with chronic disease or HIV-associated anemia

Inflammatory Markers.clinical_significance: Mildly elevated — modest inflammatory response consistent with chronic/subacute infection in immunocompromised host
Mildly elevated — non-specific, can be seen in HIV, lymphoma, and disseminated infections

HIV and Immunology.clinical_significance: Positive HIV screen — confirmatory testing follows
Very high viral load consistent with untreated HIV with advanced immunosuppression
Severely depleted CD4 count (<200 = AIDS; <100 = high risk for cryptococcal and other opportunistic infections)
Markedly elevated serum cryptococcal antigen confirms disseminated cryptococcal disease. High titer correlates with high fungal burden.

Hepatic Panel.clinical_significance: Low albumin consistent with chronic illness and malnutrition

labs.interpretation: Laboratory results reveal pancytopenia (leukopenia with WBC 3.2, anemia with Hgb 10.8, low-normal platelets) with mildly elevated inflammatory markers. The critical finding is newly diagnosed HIV-1 infection with a CD4 count of 45 cells/uL and viral load of 285,000 copies/mL, confirming AIDS. Serum cryptococcal antigen is markedly positive at 1:1024, indicating disseminated cryptococcal disease. The low CRP (18) relative to the severity of disease is consistent with the impaired immune response in AIDS — the patient cannot mount a robust inflammatory response. LDH is mildly elevated. Hepatic panel is normal at baseline (important before starting antifungal and antiretroviral therapy).

special_tests.Gram_stain: No organisms seen on Gram stain
special_tests.India_ink: Positive — encapsulated budding yeast forms identified
special_tests.Fungal_culture: Pending (inoculated on Sabouraud dextrose agar and BHI agar)

csf.interpretation: CSF analysis reveals markedly elevated opening pressure (38 cmH2O) — the hallmark finding of cryptococcal meningitis and the primary driver of morbidity and mortality. The CSF is deceptively clear despite active infection, reflecting the severely impaired immune response (CD4 45) that fails to mount a robust inflammatory reaction. The mild pleocytosis (WBC 35, 90% lymphocytes) with only mildly elevated protein (78 mg/dL) and borderline low glucose (ratio 0.34) is classic for cryptococcal meningitis in AIDS — the minimal CSF inflammation belies the severity of infection. India ink preparation is positive, showing encapsulated budding yeast. CSF cryptococcal antigen is markedly positive at 1:2048 (higher than serum at 1:1024). AFB smear is negative. These findings confirm cryptococcal meningoencephalitis.

Fungal Culture.value: Cryptococcus neoformans var. grubii (molecular serotype A) isolated at 5 days
Fungal Culture.clinical_significance: Culture confirmation of C. neoformans var. grubii, the most common variety causing meningitis in HIV/AIDS. Serotype A is globally the most prevalent.

Fungal Culture.value: Amphotericin B MIC 0.25 mcg/mL (susceptible), flucytosine MIC 4 mcg/mL (susceptible), fluconazole MIC 2 mcg/mL (susceptible)
Fungal Culture.clinical_significance: Fully susceptible to standard antifungal regimen

output.interpretation: CSF fungal culture has grown Cryptococcus neoformans var. grubii at 5 days, confirming cryptococcal meningoencephalitis. The organism is fully susceptible to amphotericin B, flucytosine, and fluconazole. Standard induction therapy with amphotericin B deoxycholate (0.7-1.0 mg/kg/day) plus flucytosine (100 mg/kg/day divided q6h) for 2 weeks is appropriate, followed by consolidation with fluconazole 400 mg daily for 8 weeks, then maintenance fluconazole 200 mg daily.
abnormal_values_summary: CSF fungal culture: Cryptococcus neoformans var. grubii isolated at 5 days

special_tests.Patient_response: Significant headache improvement immediately after procedure. Visual obscurations resolved.

output.interpretation: Therapeutic lumbar puncture performed for management of elevated ICP. Opening pressure was 42 cmH2O (critically elevated). 25 mL of clear CSF was removed, reducing closing pressure to 20 cmH2O (target <20). The patient reported immediate and significant headache relief, and visual obscurations resolved. CSF parameters are similar to the initial LP. Serial therapeutic LPs should be performed daily or every other day with a target closing pressure of <20 cmH2O. If pressure remains persistently elevated despite serial LPs, a temporary lumbar drain or ventriculoperitoneal shunt should be considered.

Ophthalmology Consultation.value: Grade II papilledema — blurred disc margins, elevation of the optic disc, peripapillary hemorrhage (one flame hemorrhage). No cotton-wool spots. No retinal cryptococcomas.
Ophthalmology Consultation.clinical_significance: Papilledema from elevated ICP secondary to cryptococcal meningitis. Requires urgent ICP management to prevent permanent visual loss.

Ophthalmology Consultation.value: Grade II papilledema — similar to right eye. Blurred disc margins with disc elevation. No retinal lesions. No cryptococcomas.

Ophthalmology Consultation.clinical_significance: Enlarged blind spots are an early sign of papilledema-related visual compromise

output.interpretation: Ophthalmology confirms bilateral grade II papilledema with peripapillary hemorrhage in the right eye, enlarged blind spots bilaterally, and mildly reduced visual acuity (20/30 bilaterally). Visual acuity is preserved but at risk. No retinal cryptococcomas identified. These findings underscore the critical importance of aggressive ICP management through serial therapeutic lumbar punctures to prevent irreversible optic nerve damage. Visual fields and acuity should be monitored serially.

findings.location: Bilateral lung fields, predominantly lower lobes

signal_characteristics.CT: Solid, non-calcified, well-defined nodules. No cavitation. No ground-glass halos.

output.impression: 1. Multiple scattered small non-calcified pulmonary nodules bilaterally, consistent with disseminated cryptococcosis in the setting of confirmed cryptococcal meningitis and AIDS. 2. Mild mediastinal lymphadenopathy. The pulmonary findings confirm disseminated cryptococcal disease.

differential_by_imaging.rationale: Multiple small nodules in AIDS patient with confirmed cryptococcal meningitis — most likely disseminated crypto
KS nodules tend to be peribronchovascular with associated lymphadenopathy, but clinical context favors crypto
Miliary TB nodules are typically 1-3 mm and diffusely uniform; these are larger and more scattered

recommended_actions: Consider bronchoscopy with BAL only if diagnostic uncertainty persists

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Cryptococcal meningoencephalitis in the setting of undiagnosed HIV/AIDS

differential.key_distinguishing: Positive cryptococcal antigen and India ink confirm crypto. CSF glucose ratio is higher than typical TBM (0.34 vs usually <0.3 in TBM). Minimal inflammatory response (WBC 35) favors crypto over TB, which typically produces more CSF inflammation (WBC >100).
No mass lesion on MRI. CSF cytology pending but clinical picture is clearly infectious with positive cryptococcal antigen. CNS lymphoma typically presents with enhancing mass lesions.
No ring-enhancing mass lesions on MRI. Toxoplasmosis presents with focal neurological deficits and characteristic ring-enhancing lesions, not diffuse meningitis.

4-week indolent course with minimal fever, lymphocytic CSF with only 35 WBC, and afebrile presentation exclude typical bacterial meningitis. Bacterial meningitis is hyperacute with neutrophilic predominance.

optimal_actions.action: Perform lumbar puncture with opening pressure measurement — ICP management is the key to survival in cryptococcal meningitis
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Markedly elevated opening pressure (38 cmH2O), clear CSF with mild lymphocytic pleocytosis, positive India ink and cryptococcal antigen

optimal_actions.action: Test CSF for cryptococcal antigen AND India ink preparation
optimal_actions.expected_finding: India ink positive, CSF CrAg positive at 1:2048

optimal_actions.action: Test for HIV immediately — cryptococcal meningitis is an AIDS-defining illness
optimal_actions.expected_finding: HIV-1 positive, CD4 45 cells/uL, viral load 285,000

optimal_actions.action: Start induction antifungal therapy: amphotericin B deoxycholate (0.7-1.0 mg/kg/day) plus flucytosine (100 mg/kg/day divided q6h) for 2 weeks
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Induction therapy to rapidly reduce fungal burden

optimal_actions.action: Perform serial therapeutic LPs to manage elevated ICP — goal closing pressure <20 cmH2O
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Pressure reduction from 42 to 20 cmH2O with immediate symptom relief

optimal_actions.action: Do NOT start ART immediately — wait 4-6 weeks after starting antifungal therapy to avoid immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome (IRIS)
optimal_actions.expected_finding: ART initiation deferred to reduce risk of fatal IRIS

optimal_actions.action: Obtain ophthalmology consultation to assess papilledema and monitor for visual compromise
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Bilateral papilledema requiring aggressive ICP management

optimal_actions.action: Obtain chest CT to evaluate for disseminated cryptococcosis
Transition to consolidation fluconazole 400 mg daily for 8 weeks after 2-week induction, then maintenance fluconazole 200 mg daily
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Step-down antifungal therapy per WHO/IDSA guidelines

optimal_actions.action: Monitor renal function and electrolytes closely during amphotericin B therapy

critical_actions: Perform LP with opening pressure measurement — elevated ICP management is the key to survival
Test CSF for cryptococcal antigen AND India ink
Test for HIV immediately — cryptococcal meningitis is an AIDS-defining illness
Start induction antifungal therapy (amphotericin B + flucytosine for 2 weeks)
Serial therapeutic LPs to manage elevated ICP (goal: OP <20 cmH2O)
Do NOT start ART immediately — wait 4-6 weeks to avoid immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome (IRIS)
contraindicated_actions: Starting ART immediately (risk of fatal IRIS)
Prescribing corticosteroids (no benefit in cryptococcal meningitis, unlike TB meningitis)
Relying on neck stiffness to diagnose meningitis (often absent in crypto)
Treating empirically with antibacterials without CSF analysis in a subacute presentation
key_reasoning_points: Subacute/chronic headache (4 weeks) with minimal fever in an immunocompromised patient = think opportunistic infection
Oral thrush + wasting + leukopenia in a sexually active man who hasn't seen a doctor in 8 years = high suspicion for undiagnosed HIV
Markedly elevated opening pressure (38 cmH2O) is THE hallmark of cryptococcal meningitis and the main cause of morbidity/mortality
Deceptively clear CSF with minimal pleocytosis (WBC 35) is classic for crypto in AIDS — the immune system cannot mount a robust inflammatory response
Absent meningismus in ~50% of cryptococcal meningitis cases — absence of neck stiffness does NOT exclude meningitis
Gelatinous pseudocysts in basal ganglia on MRI are characteristic of cryptococcal infection
ART must be DELAYED 4-6 weeks after starting antifungals to prevent IRIS, which can be fatal in CNS crypto

red_herrings.intended_effect: May lead clinician to exclude meningitis
red_herrings.correct_interpretation: Meningismus is often absent in cryptococcal meningitis — a well-known clinical pitfall
Cryptococcal meningitis in AIDS often presents without significant fever due to impaired immune response

red_herrings.intended_effect: Clear CSF may falsely reassure that meningitis is absent
red_herrings.correct_interpretation: CSF can be clear in cryptococcal meningitis due to minimal inflammatory cell response in severely immunocompromised patients

metadata.diagnostic_challenge: Chronic meningitis in a patient with undiagnosed HIV/AIDS. Absent meningismus, minimal fever, clear CSF, and only mild pleocytosis may mislead the clinician. Markedly elevated ICP is the critical finding requiring urgent management.
clinical_pearls: Elevated ICP management (serial therapeutic LPs) is the single most important intervention in cryptococcal meningitis survival
CSF cryptococcal antigen has >95% sensitivity and >95% specificity — it is the gold standard rapid diagnostic test
ART initiation must be delayed 4-6 weeks in CNS cryptococcosis to prevent fatal IRIS (COAT trial, NEJM 2014)
Corticosteroids are harmful in cryptococcal meningitis — unlike TB meningitis where dexamethasone saves lives
Gelatinous pseudocysts in basal ganglia are pathognomonic MRI findings for CNS cryptococcosis
